text: You see now, my friend? Or if you do not, I will add this. Mrs. Rupert Carrington was, before her marriage, Flossie Halliday, daughter of old man Halliday, the steel king of America.
context: Poirot explains to Hastings why Halliday's note concerns the train murder.

text: I did him a little service in the past—an affair of bearer bonds. And once, when I was in Paris for a royal visit, I had Mademoiselle Flossie pointed out to me. *La jolie petite pensionnaire!* She had the *jolie dot* too! It caused trouble.
context: Poirot recalls his prior connection to Halliday and Flossie.

text: A certain Count de la Rochefour. *Un bien mauvais sujet!* A bad hat, as you would say. An adventurer pure and simple, who knew how to appeal to a romantic young girl. Luckily her father got wind of it in time.
context: Poirot describes the Count's character and influence on Flossie.

text: Ah, the poor little lady! *Elle n'est pas bien tombée!*
context: Poirot reacts sympathetically to Flossie's unhappy marriage.

text: Old man Halliday is no fool. He would tie up her money pretty tight.
context: Poirot predicts that Halliday protected his daughter's fortune.

text: My good friend, do not jump down my throat like that. You are interested, I see. Supposing you accompany me to see Mr. Halliday. There is a taxi stand at the corner.
context: Poirot calms Hastings and invites him to meet Halliday.

text: I accept, monsieur, all the more willingly that I saw your daughter in Paris several times. And now I will ask you to tell me the circumstances of her journey to Plymouth and any other details that seem to you to bear upon the case.
context: Poirot accepts Halliday's case and requests the relevant facts.

text: Who had charge of the jewels? Your daughter, or the maid?
context: Poirot interrupts Halliday to establish who carried the jewels.

text: Is there nothing to account for your daughter's sudden change of plan?
context: Poirot asks Halliday what might explain Flossie's altered journey.

text: The train was a corridor one, of course?
context: Poirot checks the physical arrangement of the train.

text: Which side was the corridor?
context: Poirot asks which side of the carriage the corridor occupied.

text: It affects my nerves to see anything crooked. Strange, is it not? I was saying, monsieur, that there is no doubt in your mind, as to this probably unexpected meeting being the cause of your daughter's sudden change of plan?
context: After straightening an inkstand, Poirot resumes questioning Halliday.

text: One thing more, monsieur. Your daughter's fortune—to whom does it pass at her death?
context: Poirot asks Halliday who benefits financially from Flossie's death.

text: You will permit me to put a few questions? Your mistress, she was quite as usual before starting yesterday morning? Not excited or flurried?
context: Poirot begins interviewing Jane Mason about Flossie's behavior.

text: It was not your master, Mr. Carrington, by any chance?
context: Poirot asks whether the unidentified man could have been Carrington.

text: Would it be possible for the man to have entered the train at Bristol before you reached the carriage?
context: Poirot tests Jane Mason's account of the mysterious man's arrival.

text: The papers give a few details, but I would like you to confirm them.
context: Poirot asks Jane Mason to verify Flossie's clothing.

text: *Précisément!* —Thank you, mademoiselle.
context: Poirot acknowledges the clothing description and dismisses the maid politely.

text: That is all I can do here—except, monsieur, that I would ask you to tell me everything—but *everything*!
context: Poirot presses Halliday to disclose every relevant fact.

text: Then there is nothing more to be said. I must decline the case.
context: Poirot threatens to withdraw when Halliday insists he has told everything.

text: Because you have not been frank with me.
context: Poirot explains why he will not proceed without full disclosure.

text: No, you are keeping something back.
context: Poirot directly challenges Halliday's concealment.

text: I fancy, monsieur, that you did not know that your daughter intended renewing her acquaintance with the Count de la Rochefour?
context: Poirot confronts Halliday with the significance of the hidden letter.

text: Monsieur, I did not. But to track footmarks, and recognize cigarette-ash is not sufficient for a detective. He must also be a good psychologist! I knew that you disliked and mistrusted your son-in-law. He benefits by your daughter's death; the maid's description of the mysterious man bears a sufficient resemblance to him.
context: Poirot explains how psychology revealed that Halliday was withholding evidence.

text: Yet you are not keen on his track! Why? Surely because your suspicions lie in another direction. Therefore you were keeping something back.
context: Poirot completes his inference from Halliday's reluctance to accuse Carrington.

text: Yes. The Count says: 'Before very long, and perhaps sooner than you think.' Obviously he would not want to wait until you should get wind of his reappearance. Was it he who traveled down from London by the twelve-fourteen, and came along the corridor to your daughter's compartment? The Count de la Rochefour is also, if I remember rightly, tall and dark!
context: Poirot considers whether the Count arranged the meeting on the train.

text: Well, monsieur, I will wish you good day. Scotland Yard, has, I presume, a list of the jewels?
context: Poirot closes the interview and checks whether police have the jewel list.

text: They function, my good Japp; assuredly they do!
context: Poirot answers Japp's teasing question about his little gray cells.

text: A great precaution, but perhaps a day late,
context: Poirot gently mocks Japp's decision to watch Carrington.

text: You will come round and see me this evening, and tell me the result?
context: Poirot asks Japp to report the results of his inquiries.

text: He travels; he measures footprints; he collects mud and cigarette-ash! He is extremely busy! He is zealous beyond words! And if I mentioned psychology to him, do you know what he would do, my friend?
context: Poirot contrasts Japp's physical investigations with his own psychological method.

text: He would smile! He would say to himself: 'Poor old Poirot! He ages! He grows senile!'
context: Poirot imagines Japp dismissing his psychological approach.

text: Japp is the 'younger generation knocking on the door.' And *ma foi!* They are so busy knocking that they do not notice that the door is open!
context: Poirot jokes to Hastings about Japp and the younger generation.

text: As we have *carte blanche*, I shall expend threepence in ringing up the Ritz—where you may have noticed our Count is staying. After that, as my feet are a little damp, and I have sneezed twice, I shall return to my rooms and make myself a *tisano* over the spirit lamp!
context: Poirot states his next investigative step and his concern for his health.

text: Because the Count de la Rochefour has left the Ritz? You go too fast, my friend.
context: Poirot cautions Hastings against clearing Carrington too quickly.

text: One hundred thousand dollars' worth of jewelry is a very good motive for anyone. No, the question to my mind is: why kill her? Why not simply steal the jewels? She would not prosecute.
context: Poirot distinguishes the motive for theft from the motive for murder.

text: Because she is a woman, *mon ami*. She once loved this man. Therefore she would suffer her loss in silence. And the Count, who is an extremely good psychologist where women are concerned,—hence his successes,—would know that perfectly well! On the other hand, if Rupert Carrington killed her, why take the jewels, which would incriminate him fatally?
context: Poirot reasons about Flossie's likely response to theft and the suspects' motives.

text: Me, I have arranged my ideas,
context: Poirot tells Japp that he has organized his thoughts.

text: *Quel dommage?*
context: Poirot responds ironically to Japp's remark about active young investigators.

text: You permit me to make a guess? You have found the knife with which the crime was committed by the side of the line between Weston and Taunton, and you have interviewed the paper-boy who spoke to Mrs. Carrington at Weston!
context: Poirot accurately predicts the results of Japp's investigation.

text: I am glad you admit for once that they are *all mighty*! Tell me, did she give the paper-boy a shilling for himself?
context: Poirot jokes about his gray cells and tests a detail of the paper-boy encounter.

text: And in consequence the boy did not forget her?
context: Poirot links the large tip to the paper-boy's clear memory.

text: I don't know—except that he was a short man.
context: Poirot infers the jewel passer's build before Japp names him.

text: Hastings, my hat. And the brush. So! My galoshes if it still rains!
context: Poirot directs Hastings to prepare his clothes before leaving.

text: We must not undo the good work of that *tisano*. Au revoir, Japp!
context: Poirot protects his health and takes leave of Japp.

text: Ask Mr. Halliday if he will be so kind as to mount to me here,
context: Poirot politely sends the footman to fetch Halliday upstairs.

text: I was looking, monsieur, for *this*.
context: Poirot produces the duplicate blue clothes from the maid's trunk.

text: If you will just shut the door, Hastings. Thank you. Yes, and stand with your back against it. Now, Mr. Halliday, let me introduce you to Grace Kidd, otherwise Jane Mason, who will shortly rejoin her accomplice, Red Narky, under the kind escort of Japp.
context: Poirot secures the room and reveals Jane Mason's true identity.

text: It was of the most simple.
context: Poirot characterizes his solution after exposing the accomplice.

text: It was the maid's insistence on the clothes that her mistress was wearing that first struck me. Why was she so anxious that our attention should be directed to them? I reflected that we had only the maid's word for the mysterious man in the carriage at Bristol. As far as the doctor's evidence went, Mrs. Carrington might easily have been murdered *before* reaching Bristol.
context: Poirot explains the first clues that made him distrust the maid's story.

text: But if so, then the maid must be an accomplice. And if she were an accomplice, she would not wish this point to rest on her evidence alone. The clothes Mrs. Carrington was wearing were of a striking nature. A maid usually has a good deal of choice as to what her mistress shall wear. Now if, after Bristol, anyone saw a lady in a bright blue coat and skirt, and a fur toque, he will be quite ready to swear he has seen Mrs. Carrington.
context: Poirot explains how the distinctive clothes were used to create false eyewitness evidence.

text: I began to reconstruct. The maid would provide herself with duplicate clothes. She and her accomplice chloroform and stab Mrs. Carrington between London and Bristol, probably taking advantage of a tunnel. Her body is rolled under the seat; the maid takes her place. At Weston she must make herself noticed.
context: Poirot reconstructs the murder and the maid's impersonation.

text: How? In all probability, a newspaper-boy will be selected. She will insure his remembering her by giving him a large tip. She also drew his attention to the color of her dress by a remark about one of the magazines. After leaving Weston, she throws the knife out of the window to mark the place where the crime presumably occurred, and changes her clothes, or buttons a long mackintosh over them.
context: Poirot explains how the disguised maid created a memorable sighting and false crime location.

text: At Taunton she leaves the train and returns to Bristol as soon as possible, where her accomplice has duly left the luggage in the cloak-room. He hands over the ticket and himself returns to London. She waits on the platform, carrying out her rôle, goes to a hotel for the night and returns to town in the morning exactly as she said.
context: Poirot completes the reconstruction of the accomplices' movements.

text: When Japp returned from his expedition, he confirmed all my deductions. He also told me that a well-known crook was passing the jewels. I knew that whoever it was would be the exact opposite of the man Jane Mason described. When I heard that it was Red Narky, who always worked with Gracie Kidd—well, I knew just where to find her.
context: Poirot explains how Japp's findings identified Jane Mason as Gracie Kidd.

text: The more I thought of it, the more I was convinced that he had nothing to do with it. That gentleman is much too careful of his own skin to risk murder. It would be out of keeping with his character.
context: Poirot clears the Count based on his assessment of the man's character.

text: The good Japp, he shall get the official credit, all right, but though he has got his Gracie Kidd, I think that I, as the Americans say, have got his goat!
context: Poirot privately jokes to Hastings about Japp receiving the official credit.
